items.prompt_md: Why does the bounded relaxation loop copy `dist` into `new_dist` before relaxing each round, instead of updating `dist` in place?
options: So a single round can't chain two edges together through a node it just updated, which would sneak in an extra hop
To save memory during the loop
Because Python's `for` loop requires a fresh list each iteration
To make the relaxation run faster
items.answer: So a single round can't chain two edges together through a node it just updated, which would sneak in an extra hop
items.explanation_md: Relaxing off a snapshot of the previous round's distances guarantees round i only ever adds paths using at most i edges; updating in place would let one round secretly use two hops.

items.prompt_md: Why can't Dijkstra directly answer "cheapest path using at most k stops"?
options: Dijkstra finalizes a node's distance the first time it's popped and never revisits it, but the cheapest route within the hop budget might need a costlier route with fewer hops
Dijkstra can't handle graphs with more than one target node
Dijkstra requires the graph to be unweighted
Dijkstra's heap can't store hop counts alongside distances
items.answer: Dijkstra finalizes a node's distance the first time it's popped and never revisits it, but the cheapest route within the hop budget might need a costlier route with fewer hops
items.explanation_md: Dijkstra's greedy "finalize on first pop" argument has no notion of a hop budget — it assumes cheaper always wins outright, which isn't true once a stop limit is imposed.

items.prompt_md: Why does the bounded relaxation loop run exactly `k + 1` rounds when the limit is `k` stops?
options: A path with k intermediate stops uses k + 1 edges, and round i guarantees correctness for paths of at most i edges
Because k + 1 rounds is always enough for Bellman-Ford to converge on any graph
Because the heap needs k + 1 pops to empty completely
The round count is arbitrary and any number would work equally well
items.answer: A path with k intermediate stops uses k + 1 edges, and round i guarantees correctness for paths of at most i edges
items.explanation_md: Each round of full-edge relaxation extends the guaranteed-correct path length by exactly one edge, so k + 1 rounds exactly covers k stops (k + 1 edges).

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of relaxing every edge for `k + 1` rounds on a graph with `E` edges?
options: O(E)
O(k * E)
O(E log V)
items.answer: O(k * E)
items.explanation_md: Each of the k + 1 rounds does one full pass over all E edges, so the total work is O(k * E).

items.prompt_md: If there were no hop limit at all and you wanted the true shortest paths (possibly with negative edges but no negative cycle), how many relaxation rounds does full Bellman-Ford need in the worst case?
options: V
E
log V
items.explanation_md: Any simple shortest path visits at most V - 1 edges (it can't repeat a node without a cycle), so V - 1 rounds of full relaxation are always enough to converge.

items.prompt_md: How do you detect a negative-weight cycle using Bellman-Ford, rather than silently returning a wrong answer?
options: Run one additional relaxation round after the normal V - 1 (or k + 1); if any distance still improves, a negative cycle affects it
Check whether any single edge weight is negative before starting
Negative cycles are impossible in any graph Bellman-Ford is applied to
Run Dijkstra afterward and compare the two results
items.answer: Run one additional relaxation round after the normal V - 1 (or k + 1); if any distance still improves, a negative cycle affects it
items.explanation_md: If distances haven't stabilized by the round where they're guaranteed to (V - 1 rounds for the unbounded case), the only explanation left is a negative cycle that keeps lowering some distance forever.

items.prompt_md: Why does the relaxation condition explicitly check `dist[u] != inf` before comparing `dist[u] + w` against `new_dist[v]`?
options: Without it, an unreachable node's placeholder infinity plus a finite edge weight could still corrupt comparisons for other still-unreachable nodes
It has no real effect and is purely stylistic
It prevents negative edge weights from ever being relaxed
It is required to keep the heap balanced
items.answer: Without it, an unreachable node's placeholder infinity plus a finite edge weight could still corrupt comparisons for other still-unreachable nodes
items.explanation_md: Relaxing from a node that hasn't actually been reached yet would treat "infinity plus something finite" as a real candidate distance, which is never correct.

items.prompt_md: Why not just DFS every path with at most k edges and take the cheapest one?
options: The number of such paths can grow exponentially in k, while bounded Bellman-Ford does the same job in O(k * E)
DFS can never visit the same node twice, making it inherently faster than Bellman-Ford
DFS and Bellman-Ford always have identical complexity
DFS doesn't work on graphs with weighted edges
items.answer: The number of such paths can grow exponentially in k, while bounded Bellman-Ford does the same job in O(k * E)
items.explanation_md: Enumerating every path up to k edges branches exponentially in the worst case; relaxing every edge k + 1 times reuses shared work instead of re-deriving each path from scratch.

items.prompt_md: What is the space complexity of the bounded Bellman-Ford relaxation, using the two-array (`dist` / `new_dist`) snapshot approach?
options: O(V)
O(V * k)
O(E)
items.answer: O(V)
items.explanation_md: Only two distance arrays of size V are ever alive at once — the previous round's snapshot and the round being built — regardless of how many rounds k + 1 is.

items.prompt_md: If `k` is at least `V - 1`, what does the bounded relaxation loop effectively compute?
options: The same result as unrestricted shortest paths (full Bellman-Ford), since any simple shortest path uses at most V - 1 edges
A wrong answer, because the loop was only designed for small k
Nothing meaningful — the loop would need to be rewritten entirely for large k
The same result as Dijkstra, since more rounds means it starts finalizing nodes permanently
items.answer: The same result as unrestricted shortest paths (full Bellman-Ford), since any simple shortest path uses at most V - 1 edges
items.explanation_md: Once the round budget reaches V - 1, no further round can ever improve a distance (no simple path needs more edges than that), so it matches unbounded Bellman-Ford.

items.prompt_md: A flight network has weighted edges, and you must find the cheapest price from a source to a destination using at most k stops. Which approach fits?
options: Push `(0, source)` onto a min-heap and pop the smallest cumulative distance (Dijkstra)
Relax every edge exactly k + 1 times, snapshotting distances between rounds so each round adds at most one hop
BFS layer by layer, counting hops only and ignoring edge weight
Try every ordering of intermediate airports
items.answer: Relax every edge exactly k + 1 times, snapshotting distances between rounds so each round adds at most one hop
items.explanation_md: A hop/stop ceiling is the fingerprint that rules out Dijkstra; bounded Bellman-Ford relaxation is built to answer exactly this question.

items.prompt_md: An unweighted graph where you just need the fewest hops from source to target, no cost involved and no cap on hops. Which approach fits?
options: Plain BFS
Bounded Bellman-Ford relaxation
Dijkstra's algorithm
Prim's algorithm
items.answer: Plain BFS
items.explanation_md: With no weights and no budget, this is the simplest possible shortest-path question — plain BFS answers it directly without any relaxation machinery.

items.prompt_md: A weighted graph may contain negative edges (no negative cycle), and you need shortest distances from a source to every node, with no hop limit. Which approach fits?
options: Full Bellman-Ford, relaxing every edge for V - 1 rounds
Dijkstra's algorithm with a min-heap
Prim's algorithm
Plain BFS
items.answer: Full Bellman-Ford, relaxing every edge for V - 1 rounds
items.explanation_md: Negative edges rule out Dijkstra regardless of any hop cap; unbounded Bellman-Ford (V - 1 rounds) is the correct general-purpose tool.

items.prompt_md: A weighted graph has strictly non-negative edges and no hop/stop limit at all — just the plain cheapest path from source to every node. Which approach fits best?
options: Dijkstra's algorithm with a min-heap
Bellman-Ford, relaxing every edge for V - 1 rounds
MST via Prim's algorithm
Plain BFS
items.answer: Dijkstra's algorithm with a min-heap
items.explanation_md: With non-negative weights and no hop cap, Dijkstra's O((V + E) log V) beats Bellman-Ford's O(V * E) — reach for Bellman-Ford only when negative edges or a hop budget rule out Dijkstra.

items.prompt_md: You must connect every node in a network with minimum total edge cost, with no fixed source or hop limit involved. Which approach fits?
options: Minimum spanning tree (Prim's or Kruskal's)
Bounded Bellman-Ford relaxation
Dijkstra's algorithm
Plain DFS
items.answer: Minimum spanning tree (Prim's or Kruskal's)
items.explanation_md: "Connect everything cheaply" with no source/target and no hop budget is an MST question, distinct from any bounded or unbounded shortest-path question.

items.prompt_md: You just need to know if you can reach node B from node A at all, regardless of cost or hop count. Which approach fits?
options: Plain BFS/DFS reachability check
Bounded Bellman-Ford relaxation
Prim's algorithm
Dijkstra's algorithm tracking cumulative cost
items.answer: Plain BFS/DFS reachability check
items.explanation_md: A yes/no reachability question doesn't need cost tracking or a hop budget at all — plain traversal answers it directly.

items.prompt_md: A delivery robot can use at most k roads, and some roads have negative cost (rebates). You need the cheapest route within that road budget. Which approach fits?
options: Relax every edge for at most k + 1 rounds, snapshotting between rounds
Dijkstra's algorithm, since it's the fastest shortest-path algorithm
Prim's algorithm
Plain BFS ignoring the cost
items.answer: Relax every edge for at most k + 1 rounds, snapshotting between rounds
items.explanation_md: Negative costs alone rule out Dijkstra, and the hop cap on top of that is exactly what bounded Bellman-Ford relaxation is designed to respect.

items.prompt_md: A graph may contain a negative-weight cycle, and you need to detect whether any node's shortest distance from the source is unbounded (keeps improving forever). Which approach fits?
options: Run Bellman-Ford's normal V - 1 rounds, then one extra round — if any distance still improves, that node is affected by a negative cycle
Run Dijkstra and see if it ever produces a negative number
Run Prim's algorithm and check if the tree cost is negative
Run plain BFS and check for revisited nodes
items.answer: Run Bellman-Ford's normal V - 1 rounds, then one extra round — if any distance still improves, that node is affected by a negative cycle
items.explanation_md: The extra-round trick is Bellman-Ford's standard way to detect negative cycles: anything that still improves past guaranteed convergence must be caught in one.

items.prompt_md: You need the k-th cheapest flight price in a plain list of ticket prices — there is no graph, no stops, no edges to relax at all. Which approach fits?
options: A heap (or quickselect) over the raw prices
Bounded Bellman-Ford relaxation
Dijkstra's algorithm
Prim's algorithm
items.answer: A heap (or quickselect) over the raw prices
items.explanation_md: With no graph structure at all — just "the k-th smallest value in a list" — a plain heap or quickselect answers it directly.

items.prompt_md: A weighted grid where the path cost is the worst single cell crossed (not a sum), and there's no hop limit — just minimize that bottleneck from start to end. Which approach fits?
options: A min-heap of (bottleneck-so-far, row, col), popping the smallest bottleneck each time
Bounded Bellman-Ford relaxation for a fixed number of rounds
Kruskal's algorithm over the grid cells
Plain BFS counting steps
items.answer: A min-heap of (bottleneck-so-far, row, col), popping the smallest bottleneck each time
items.explanation_md: With no hop limit, Dijkstra's finalize-on-first-pop guarantee still holds even when the combine rule is `max` instead of `+` — no need for Bellman-Ford's rounds.
